lenses.guidance: 'The LORD was with Joseph' frames the chapter as a refrain (vv 2, 3, 21, 23) — bookending success in Potiphar's house and prison alike. The repetition makes divine presence the constant under every changed circumstance.

Joseph's prosperity under Potiphar (vv 2-6) and his unjust imprisonment (vv 19-20) both serve the covenant arc. Promise advances through faithful obedience and through false accusation — God's plan does not flinch at injustice.

'How then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?' (v 9) — Joseph's refusal is theological, not merely prudential. Obey God when no one will know; trust the long arc; daily faithfulness in hidden places matters.

Innocent Joseph falsely accused on the word of a single witness (vv 13-18), bound and led to prison (v 20), foreshadows Christ falsely accused, bound, and condemned. The pattern of righteous suffering toward exaltation begins.
